title: Confit de Canard
subtitle: French duck legs cured and cooked in their own fat
excerpt: Duck legs cured overnight with salt, thyme, and garlic, then submerged in duck fat and cooked very slowly until the meat is impossibly tender and pulls cleanly from the bone. Stored in the fat, they improve for weeks.
sourceNotes: Confit de canard is from Gascony and the Périgord in southwest France, where the technique developed as a method of preservation before refrigeration. Documented in Paula Wolfert, The Cooking of South-West France (1983) and Elizabeth David, French Provincial Cooking (1960).

content.text: Confit is not a recipe for a weeknight. It is a two-day process: the duck legs cure in salt overnight, then submerge in fat for 2.5 hours at a very low temperature. The resulting legs are extraordinary, the meat is dark and yielding, falling from the bone, infused with the thyme and garlic from the cure. Finishing in a hot pan or under the grill crisps the skin back to crackling.

Duck fat is essential, not optional. It is widely available in tins and jars. Goose fat is an acceptable substitute. The fat can be re-used after straining, store it in the fridge and use it for roast potatoes or the next batch of confit.
Mix the salt, thyme, bay, garlic, and pepper. Rub all over the duck legs. Place in a dish, cover, and refrigerate for 12 to 24 hours.

Heat the oven to 140°C fan. Rinse the cure off the duck legs and pat dry. Melt the duck fat in a casserole over low heat. Submerge the duck legs in the fat. They should be fully covered; add more fat or a little water if needed. Cover and cook in the oven for 2 to 2.5 hours until the meat is very tender and pulling away from the bone.

To store: leave the legs in the fat, transfer to jars or a sealed container, and refrigerate, the fat preserves the duck. To serve immediately: remove the legs from the fat and place skin-side down in a very hot dry frying pan or under a hot grill for 4 to 5 minutes until the skin is cracking and deep brown. Serve with lentils, sautéed potatoes cooked in the duck fat, or frisée salad.

items.fix: Confit must cook at 80 to 90°C in the fat, a thermometer helps; the meat should not boil, only poach slowly
items.cause: Temperature too high; fat was simmering rather than barely trembling

items.fix: Pat the skin dry with kitchen paper before frying or grilling; a dry surface is essential for crisping
items.cause: Excess fat on the surface of the skin before finishing

content.text: Confit de canard is the signature dish of Gascony, the heart of foie gras and duck country in southwest France. The technique was a practical preservation method: duck legs cooked and submerged in their own fat keep for months at cool cellar temperatures. Today it is restaurant food and celebration food, though making it at home is straightforward if you plan ahead. It is the centrepiece of cassoulet, the long-baked bean dish of Toulouse.
